description: Stage 2 agent-level eval set. 30 questions across 3 difficulty tiers, grounded in the 77-paper efficient-inference corpus. Measures ANSWER quality (via LLM-as-judge rubric), distinct from Stage 1's retrieval recall. Difficulty = designer intent: simple (one distinctive paper answers it), medium (within-cluster synthesis of 2-3 papers), hard (cross-cluster synthesis, version chains, or gap-finding). 'gold_papers' lists the paper(s) a strong answer should draw on (by manifest title). 'key_points' are topic-level coverage hints for the completeness score, NOT gold facts; the judge verifies specifics against cited sources.

questions.question: How does GPTQ achieve 3-4 bit post-training quantization of large language models in just a few GPU hours?
gold_papers: GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-trained Transformer
questions.difficulty_rationale: Single foundational paper; distinctive name; one mechanism to explain.
key_points: layer-wise one-shot weight quantization
uses approximate second-order / Hessian information (OBQ lineage)
3-4 bit, no retraining
efficiency tricks (lazy batch updates / Cholesky) enabling few-GPU-hour runs

questions.question: How does PagedAttention prevent KV cache memory fragmentation in vLLM?
gold_papers: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention
questions.difficulty_rationale: Single paper, two distinctive named components (PagedAttention, vLLM).
key_points: KV cache stored in non-contiguous fixed-size blocks (pages)
virtual-memory / paging analogy
near-zero internal+external fragmentation
enables higher batch sizes / sharing via copy-on-write

questions.question: How does FlashAttention reduce memory I/O for the attention operation?
gold_papers: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
questions.difficulty_rationale: Single paper; must not drift into FA2/FA3 which are also in corpus.
key_points: IO-aware, tiling/blocking to keep data in SRAM
avoids materializing the full N×N attention matrix in HBM
online softmax / recomputation in backward
exact (not approximate) attention

questions.question: What does AWQ identify as the small fraction of salient weights, and how does it protect them during quantization?
gold_papers: AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression and Acceleration
questions.difficulty_rationale: Single paper; key concept (salient weights, activation-aware) is distinctive.
key_points: salient weights identified by activation magnitude, not weight magnitude
~1% of weights matter most
per-channel scaling to protect them (no mixed precision)
preserves accuracy at low bit-width

questions.question: How does SmoothQuant migrate quantization difficulty from activations to weights?
gold_papers: SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Quantization for Large Language Models
questions.difficulty_rationale: Single paper; the migration idea is its signature.
key_points: activation outliers make activation quantization hard
mathematically equivalent per-channel scaling shifts difficulty W↔A
enables W8A8
offline migration factor (alpha)

questions.question: How does Medusa accelerate LLM decoding using multiple decoding heads?
gold_papers: Medusa: Simple LLM Inference Acceleration Framework with Multiple Decoding Heads
questions.difficulty_rationale: Single paper; distinctive mechanism.
key_points: extra decoding heads predict multiple future tokens in parallel
tree-based candidate generation + verification
no separate draft model needed
typical acceptance via tree attention

questions.question: How does H2O (Heavy-Hitter Oracle) decide which KV cache entries to keep or evict?
gold_papers: H$_2$O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
questions.difficulty_rationale: Single paper; heavy-hitter concept is distinctive.
key_points: small set of 'heavy hitter' tokens dominate attention
eviction policy keeps heavy hitters + recent tokens
based on accumulated attention scores
large KV cache reduction with low quality loss

questions.question: What is the core idea behind 1.58-bit LLMs (BitNet b1.58)?
gold_papers: The Era of 1-bit LLMs: All Large Language Models are in 1.58 Bits
questions.difficulty_rationale: Single paper; ternary-weight idea is unmistakable.
key_points: ternary weights {-1, 0, 1} => log2(3) ≈ 1.58 bits
trained from scratch (quantization-aware), not PTQ
matches FP baseline at scale
removes most multiplications / hardware-efficiency implications

questions.question: How does StreamingLLM use attention sinks to support effectively infinite-length streaming generation?
gold_papers: Efficient Streaming Language Models with Attention Sinks
questions.difficulty_rationale: Single paper; 'attention sink' is its signature finding.
key_points: initial tokens act as attention sinks absorbing excess attention
keep first few tokens + sliding recent window
fixes perplexity collapse when evicting early tokens
no fine-tuning, constant memory

questions.question: How does QLoRA's 4-bit NormalFloat enable fine-tuning a 65B-parameter model on a single GPU?
gold_papers: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
questions.difficulty_rationale: Single paper; NF4 + LoRA combo is distinctive.
key_points: 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4) information-theoretically optimal for normal weights
double quantization of constants
paged optimizers to handle memory spikes
frozen 4-bit base + trainable LoRA adapters

questions.question: How do SmoothQuant and AWQ differ in their approach to the activation-outlier problem in LLM quantization?
gold_papers: SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Quantization for Large Language Models
AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression and Acceleration
questions.difficulty_rationale: Two papers in same cluster; requires contrasting mechanisms, not just describing one.
key_points: SmoothQuant targets W8A8 by migrating outlier difficulty W↔A
AWQ is weight-only, uses activation stats to find salient weights
both 'activation-aware' but different goals (activation quant vs weight quant)
scaling used differently

questions.question: What changed across FlashAttention-1, FlashAttention-2, and FlashAttention-3 in terms of parallelism and hardware utilization?
gold_papers: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
FlashAttention-2: Faster Attention with Better Parallelism and Work Partitioning
FlashAttention-3: Fast and Accurate Attention with Asynchrony and Low-precision
questions.difficulty_rationale: Three-paper version chain; must attribute each change to the right version.
key_points: FA1: IO-awareness, tiling, SRAM
FA2: better work partitioning (split-Q), seq-len parallelism, fewer non-matmul FLOPs
FA3: asynchrony (warp-specialization), FP8/low-precision, Hopper-specific
utilization gains across versions

questions.question: Compare the KV-cache eviction strategies of H2O, SnapKV, and Scissorhands. What signal does each use to decide what to keep?
gold_papers: H$_2$O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
SnapKV: LLM Knows What You are Looking for Before Generation
Scissorhands: Exploiting the Persistence of Importance Hypothesis for LLM KV Cache Compression at Test Time
questions.difficulty_rationale: Three within-cluster papers; easy to conflate; needs the distinguishing signal of each.
key_points: H2O: accumulated attention scores (heavy hitters) + recent
SnapKV: uses observation window / prompt attention to select before generation
Scissorhands: persistence-of-importance hypothesis across steps
prompt-time vs decode-time selection differences

questions.question: How do EAGLE and Medusa differ in how they produce draft tokens for speculative decoding?
gold_papers: EAGLE: Speculative Sampling Requires Rethinking Feature Uncertainty
Medusa: Simple LLM Inference Acceleration Framework with Multiple Decoding Heads
questions.difficulty_rationale: Two same-cluster papers; distinct drafting mechanisms.
key_points: Medusa: multiple independent heads predict future positions
EAGLE: autoregressive at the feature level, reuses second-to-top-layer features
EAGLE addresses feature uncertainty / better acceptance
tree verification common to both

questions.question: How do Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) and Multi-Query Attention trade off model quality against KV-cache size?
gold_papers: GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models from Multi-Head Checkpoints
Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need
questions.difficulty_rationale: Two papers; the MQA↔MHA↔GQA spectrum is the point.
key_points: MQA: single shared K/V head => smallest KV cache, larger quality drop
GQA: groups of query heads share K/V => interpolates MHA↔MQA
GQA recovers most quality with much smaller cache
uptraining from MHA checkpoints

questions.question: DistServe and Splitwise both separate prefill from decode. How does each disaggregate these phases, and why?
gold_papers: DistServe: Disaggregating Prefill and Decoding for Goodput-optimized Large Language Model Serving
Splitwise: Efficient generative LLM inference using phase splitting
questions.difficulty_rationale: Two same-cluster papers; similar idea, different motivations/hardware angle.
key_points: prefill is compute-bound, decode is memory-bound
DistServe: goodput optimization, separate resource allocation per phase + SLO
Splitwise: phase splitting across (possibly heterogeneous) machines
KV cache transfer between phases

questions.question: How do Linformer and Performer each achieve linear-complexity attention, and how do their approximations differ?
gold_papers: Linformer: Self-Attention with Linear Complexity
Rethinking Attention with Performers
questions.difficulty_rationale: Two papers; both 'linear attention' but fundamentally different math.
key_points: Linformer: low-rank projection of keys/values (n→k)
Performer: kernel/random-feature (FAVOR+) approximation of softmax
Linformer assumes low-rank; Performer gives unbiased kernel estimate
different failure modes / guarantees

questions.question: What does QuIP# add over the original QuIP to push toward near-lossless 2-bit weight quantization?
gold_papers: QuIP: 2-Bit Quantization of Large Language Models With Guarantees
QuIP#: Even Better LLM Quantization with Hadamard Incoherence and Lattice Codebooks
questions.difficulty_rationale: Version pair; must isolate the incremental contributions.
key_points: QuIP: incoherence processing + adaptive rounding with guarantees
QuIP#: faster Hadamard-based incoherence, lattice (E8) codebooks
fine-tuning to recover accuracy
better 2-bit quality than QuIP

questions.question: How do Ring Attention and Striped Attention differ in handling causal masking for long-context training?
gold_papers: Ring Attention with Blockwise Transformers for Near-Infinite Context
Striped Attention: Faster Ring Attention for Causal Transformers
questions.difficulty_rationale: Version/extension pair; the causal-load-imbalance fix is subtle.
key_points: Ring Attention: blockwise attention sharded across devices in a ring, overlaps comms
causal masking creates load imbalance in Ring
Striped: permutes/stripes tokens to balance per-device work under causal mask
speedup over Ring for causal

questions.question: How do KIVI and KVQuant differ in their approach to low-bit quantization of the KV cache?
gold_papers: KIVI: A Tuning-Free Asymmetric 2bit Quantization for KV Cache
KVQuant: Towards 10 Million Context Length LLM Inference with KV Cache Quantization
questions.difficulty_rationale: Two same-cluster papers; both KV-quant but different granularity choices.
key_points: KIVI: tuning-free 2-bit, per-channel for keys, per-token for values
KVQuant: per-channel key quant, non-uniform / outlier handling, very long context
asymmetry between key and value distributions
different bit/accuracy/context targets

questions.question: How can speculative decoding be made effective at large batch sizes, and what are the bottlenecks?
gold_papers: MagicDec: Breaking the Latency-Throughput Tradeoff for Long Context Generation with Speculative Decoding
Hydra: Sequentially-Dependent Draft Heads for Medusa Decoding
SpecInfer: Accelerating Generative Large Language Model Serving with Tree-based Speculative Inference and Verification
Speculative Speculative Decoding
questions.difficulty_rationale: Cross-paper synthesis; requires the compute-bound vs memory-bound regime argument, not a single paper's claim.
key_points: large batch => verification becomes compute-bound (arithmetic intensity)
critical sequence length: long-seq shifts bottleneck to KV loading
draft tree size must shrink with batch size
compressed-KV self-speculation; parallelizing draft/verify

questions.question: Several methods reduce attention complexity toward linear time. What are the main families, and what wall-clock caveats apply across them?
gold_papers: Linformer: Self-Attention with Linear Complexity
Rethinking Attention with Performers
Big Bird: Transformers for Longer Sequences
Longformer: The Long-Document Transformer
Nyströmformer: A Nyström-Based Algorithm for Approximating Self-Attention
Fastformer: Additive Attention Can Be All You Need
FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
questions.difficulty_rationale: Cross-cluster taxonomy + a non-obvious caveat (FLOPs ≠ wall-clock).
key_points: low-rank (Linformer), kernel/random-feature (Performer), sparse/windowed (BigBird, Longformer), Nyström, additive (Fastformer)
linear FLOPs often don't yield wall-clock speedup (memory I/O)
FlashAttention point: exact attention can beat approximations in practice
causal-masking difficulties for some kernels

questions.question: KV-cache eviction and KV-cache quantization both shrink the cache. When would you prefer eviction (e.g. H2O, SnapKV) over quantization (e.g. KIVI, KVQuant), and can they be composed?
gold_papers: H$_2$O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
SnapKV: LLM Knows What You are Looking for Before Generation
KIVI: A Tuning-Free Asymmetric 2bit Quantization for KV Cache
KVQuant: Towards 10 Million Context Length LLM Inference with KV Cache Quantization
questions.difficulty_rationale: Cross-sub-cluster synthesis + tradeoff reasoning + composition (a question no single paper fully answers).
key_points: eviction drops tokens (lossy in token dimension); quant keeps all tokens at lower precision
eviction risks dropping later-needed context; quant preserves coverage
memory-savings vs accuracy tradeoffs differ
composition: evict then quantize the survivors (orthogonal axes) — reason about it even if no paper states it

questions.question: How does the compute-bound vs memory-bound (arithmetic intensity) distinction explain why different efficiency techniques help in different regimes of batch size and sequence length?
gold_papers: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
MagicDec: Breaking the Latency-Throughput Tradeoff for Long Context Generation with Speculative Decoding
Efficiently Scaling Transformer Inference
questions.difficulty_rationale: Connects a single physical concept across multiple papers/techniques; tests conceptual synthesis.
key_points: decode at small batch = memory-bandwidth-bound; large batch/prefill = compute-bound
FlashAttention targets memory I/O
speculative decoding 'free' only when compute is idle (low batch)
roofline / arithmetic-intensity framing ties them together

questions.question: Speculative decoding needs a cheap draft, while KV quantization changes the model's memory profile. Which KV/quantization techniques are compatible with self-speculation, and where do they conflict?
gold_papers: QuantSpec: Self-Speculative Decoding with Hierarchical Quantized KV Cache
MagicDec: Breaking the Latency-Throughput Tradeoff for Long Context Generation with Speculative Decoding
KIVI: A Tuning-Free Asymmetric 2bit Quantization for KV Cache
questions.difficulty_rationale: Gap/synthesis: requires identifying the one paper that jointly addresses it (QuantSpec) and reasoning about compatibility — high hallucination risk if the agent invents combinations.
key_points: QuantSpec: self-speculation using a hierarchical quantized KV cache as the draft
MagicDec: compressed-KV self-speculation at large batch/long context
draft must be cheaper but aligned enough for high acceptance
honest treatment of what is vs isn't directly studied

questions.question: For ultra-long-context inference, what is the combined story across attention kernels, KV-cache management, and sparse attention — and where do these approaches conflict or overlap?
gold_papers: Ring Attention with Blockwise Transformers for Near-Infinite Context
Efficient Streaming Language Models with Attention Sinks
SnapKV: LLM Knows What You are Looking for Before Generation
Big Bird: Transformers for Longer Sequences
questions.difficulty_rationale: Cross-cluster integration across three different mechanism families.
key_points: exact-but-distributed (Ring) vs sparse-approximate (BigBird) vs cache-eviction (SnapKV, StreamingLLM)
different things they trade away (compute/comm vs accuracy vs dropped tokens)
they operate at different layers and can compose
no single approach solves all of long-context

questions.question: Does the corpus contain a method that jointly quantizes BOTH the model weights and the KV cache, or is that a gap? What would combining weight quantization (e.g. GPTQ/AWQ) with KV-cache quantization (e.g. KIVI) entail?
gold_papers: GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-trained Transformer
AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression and Acceleration
KIVI: A Tuning-Free Asymmetric 2bit Quantization for KV Cache
Atom: Low-bit Quantization for Efficient and Accurate LLM Serving
KVQuant: Towards 10 Million Context Length LLM Inference with KV Cache Quantization
questions.difficulty_rationale: Gap-probe whose honest answer is 'NOT a gap': Atom genuinely combines low-bit weight/activation AND KV-cache quantization in one framework, and KIVI/KVQuant note weight-quant compatibility. Tests whether the agent finds the real joint method and reasons about the orthogonal axes WITHOUT overstating or mis-citing what each paper claims (e.g. fabricating a 'joint quantization' section).
key_points: weight-quant (GPTQ/AWQ) targets static weights; KV-quant (KIVI/KVQuant) targets the streaming KV tensor — different tensors, different methods (offline second-order calibration unsuitable for streaming KV)
NOT a gap: Atom jointly quantizes weights/activations + KV cache end-to-end; KIVI/KVQuant explicitly note compatibility with weight-only quant (orthogonal axes)
what joint application entails: independent compression axes, different outlier structures, combined/additive error budget
credit for correctly identifying the joint method (Atom) and honest scoping; penalize fabricated or mis-cited specifics

questions.question: Across the serving systems in the corpus (vLLM, Sarathi-Serve, DeepSpeed-FastGen, DistServe), what are the distinct levers for improving the throughput-latency tradeoff, and do any of them conflict?
gold_papers: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention
Taming Throughput-Latency Tradeoff in LLM Inference with Sarathi-Serve
DeepSpeed-FastGen: High-throughput Text Generation for LLMs via MII and DeepSpeed-Inference
DistServe: Disaggregating Prefill and Decoding for Goodput-optimized Large Language Model Serving
questions.difficulty_rationale: Cross-paper synthesis within serving; each contributes a different lever (memory, chunked-prefill, dynamic splitfuse, disaggregation).
key_points: vLLM: paged KV memory => batch size
Sarathi: chunked prefill to avoid stalls / balance prefill+decode
DeepSpeed-FastGen: Dynamic SplitFuse
DistServe: phase disaggregation; tension between batching for throughput vs latency SLOs

questions.question: The corpus contains EAGLE, EAGLE-2, and EAGLE-3. Trace what each version changed and what problem each addressed.
gold_papers: EAGLE: Speculative Sampling Requires Rethinking Feature Uncertainty
EAGLE-2: Faster Inference of Language Models with Dynamic Draft Trees
EAGLE-3: Scaling up Inference Acceleration of Large Language Models via Training-Time Test
questions.difficulty_rationale: Three-version evolution narrative; must keep contributions correctly ordered and not blur versions.
key_points: EAGLE: feature-level autoregression, addresses feature uncertainty
EAGLE-2: dynamic / context-aware draft trees (not static)
EAGLE-3: training-time test, removes feature-prediction constraint, scaling
each builds on the prior

questions.question: What approaches in the corpus attack the sequential-dependency bottleneck of autoregressive decoding — spanning speculative decoding and parallel/blockwise methods — and what are their fundamental limits?
gold_papers: Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding
Accelerating Large Language Model Decoding with Speculative Sampling
Medusa: Simple LLM Inference Acceleration Framework with Multiple Decoding Heads
Lookahead: An Inference Acceleration Framework for Large Language Model with Lossless Generation Accuracy
questions.difficulty_rationale: Cross-paradigm synthesis + a limits/gap discussion (acceptance rate, verification cost, memory).
key_points: speculative decoding: draft+verify, limited by acceptance rate & draft cost
parallel decoding heads / Lookahead (Jacobi-style n-gram) reduce serial steps
all are bounded by verification/compute and target-model fidelity
fundamental limit: still need target model to verify => no free lunch at high batch
